title: Protein Folding Characterization via Persistent Homology
speaker: Marcio Gameiro
abstract: We use persistent homology to analyze predictions of protein folding by trying to identify global geometric structures that contribute to the error when the protein is misfolded. The goal is to find correlations between global geometric structures, as measured by persistent homology, and the failure to predict the correct folding. This technique could be useful in guiding the energy minimization techniques to the correct minimum corresponding to the desired folding.
tags: Workshop on Topology: Identifying Order in Complex Systems

proof_stages.label: Protein-folding false-minimum problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up simulated protein folding as energy minimization, explains RMSD comparison with known PDB native states, and formulates the working hypothesis that low-energy but high-RMSD predictions may reveal global geometric defects in the energy functional.

proof_stages.label: Persistent homology as a stable shape descriptor
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls filtrations, Betti numbers, persistence diagrams, and bottleneck stability, emphasizing that persistent homology can robustly encode sizes and lifetimes of holes in geometric data.

proof_stages.label: Weighted alpha complexes for protein atom clouds
proof_stages.summary: Protein structures are converted from atom coordinates and van der Waals radii into weighted alpha-shape filtrations, whose complexes preserve the homology of unions of growing balls and can be computed efficiently from PDB-format data.

proof_stages.label: Persistence-image vectorization
proof_stages.summary: Persistence diagrams are turned into finite-dimensional vectors by placing weighted Gaussians at birth-persistence points and sampling or integrating over a chosen grid, preserving a useful stability property for downstream statistics.

proof_stages.label: Linear regression and dual persistence diagrams
proof_stages.summary: The RMSD error is modeled as a linear function of the persistence-image vector, producing an interpretable dual persistence diagram, with L2 and L1 regularization used to control overfitting and, in the L1 case, encourage sparsity.

proof_stages.label: Preliminary data analysis on low-energy predictions
proof_stages.summary: The method is applied to about 400 low-energy predicted structures from roughly 43 to 48 small protein types, and cross-validated regression scores suggest that two-dimensional persistence diagrams of cavities contain information predictive of folding error.

proof_stages.label: Tracing significant topological features back to atoms
proof_stages.summary: High-magnitude regions of the learned dual diagram, especially in the sparse L1 fit, are proposed as targets for recovering persistence generators and mapping them back to simplices and atoms that may indicate where the energy functional is failing.

examples.name: Prions as misfolded proteins
examples.note: An audience member raises prions to clarify the distinction between an alternative acceptable fold and a wrong, pathological fold; the exchange illustrates the biological meaning of a bad fold without producing the method.
examples.engagement_reason: Prions are only brought up and described as wrongly folded brain proteins, with no computation or analysis performed on them.

examples.name: PDE level sets with beta_0 equal to one

examples.engagement_reason: The PDE level sets are recalled as past simulations where beta_0 was always one, but no level set is worked through in the talk.
